lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market operators that raise cattle for both milking and meat production and repeatedly sell milk, calves, cull cows, or finished cattle to external cooperatives, processors, feeders, auctions, or packers.
lens.excluded_activities: Beef-only ranches, cattle feedlots, dairy-only farms, goat dairies, hobby farms, passive landowners, captive internal units, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring milk sales and periodic cattle sales through cooperatives, processors, livestock auctions, dealers, feeders, or packers, with the farm retaining animal-care, land, production, food-safety, and environmental accountability.

a.bridge: USDA measures technology adoption and operating effects in dairy-only and beef-ranch populations, not wage-weighted task exposure in dual-purpose farms. I map robotic milking, cow-level sensing, remote livestock and water monitoring, vegetation assessment, health alerts, breeding records, scheduling, and administrative work to exposed tasks, then haircut feeding, calving, veterinary treatment, fencing, equipment repair, manure handling, transport, and physical animal work; adjacent specialized farms may overstate capital intensity and understate task breadth at smaller dual-purpose operations.
a.rationale: The combination of milk and meat creates frequent animal, production, reproduction, health, pasture, and market data that AI can analyze. Monitoring and milking are meaningful targets, but livestock care remains continuous, physical, exception-heavy, and safety-sensitive. The supplied labor-share input is missing, so the effect cannot be converted into an industry labor opportunity without additional data.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study of commercial dual-purpose farms covering milking, feeding, herd checks, pasture and water monitoring, breeding, calving, health, maintenance, compliance, sales, and current automation would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Evidence comes from adjacent dairy and beef operations because exact-industry technology studies were not found.
Owner and family labor are material but often absent from payroll-based occupation data.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio is missing and was not replaced.

rho.rationale: Commercial sensors, robotic milking, herd-management software, remote cameras, water monitors, GPS, and decision-support tools can be adopted within five years. Implementation is limited by low connectivity, device durability, data integration, herd scale, facilities, training, animal-welfare requirements, financing, and the need for people to respond physically to alerts.
rho.change_evidence: Farm-level deployment records showing herd size, system cost, connectivity, uptime, false alerts, labor hours before and after, veterinary outcomes, and payback across mixed milk-and-meat farms would materially move the range.
caveats: USDA reports positive average returns for precision dairy technology, but economics may not transfer to smaller dual-purpose herds.
Ranching systems can identify work while still requiring travel and physical intervention.

e.rationale: Eligibility is likely limited because the exact NAICS requires material milk and meat activity, while commercial cattle operations usually specialize as beef, feedlot, or dairy businesses. Many mixed farms are small, family or lifestyle operations below the target EBITDA band; others are land-heavy, lack external recurring milk sales, or cannot transfer without family labor. A minority of scaled, professionally operated mixed herds may qualify, but the frozen firm count is missing.
e.change_evidence: USDA Census or ARMS microdata identifying NAICS 112130 operations by milk and cattle revenue, normalized EBITDA, herd, owner labor, external buyers, legal entity, and control rights would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: The exact-industry population is not separately characterized in the public USDA sources found.
Beef-farm evidence shows 90% of specialized farms below $100,000 of sales and may not describe dual-purpose operations.
The supplied LMM firm count is missing and was not estimated.

s5.bridge: The 2022 Census measures specialized beef producers, not eligible dual-purpose LMM firms: 39% were age 65 or older, average age was 58.3, and 97% of beef and feedlot farms were family farms. I use this as succession-pressure evidence and discount for intrafamily transfers, leases, partial land sales, continued operation, liquidation of cattle, and farm exit; it likely overstates qualifying external control transfers when succession remains inside families.
s5.rationale: Advanced producer age and family ownership support meaningful transition activity over five years, but cattle operations often pass within families or sell land and herd separately. Low profitability can create exits without a transferable going concern, while valuable land can encourage real-estate transactions rather than operating-company sales.
s5.change_evidence: Exact-industry owner-age and succession-plan data plus five to ten years of cattle-farm entity transfers, land-and-herd deals, USDA ownership records, county deeds, and agricultural brokerage transactions coded for external control and operating continuity would materially change the probability.
caveats: Beef-specialist demographics are an adjacent-population proxy.
Farm property sales do not necessarily transfer the operating business.
No exact-industry control-transfer series was found.

q.rationale: Milk and cattle are commodity outputs whose prices are set by cooperative, regulated-order, processor, auction, and market conditions rather than farm cost. Operators retain near-term operating savings, but competitive land and cattle markets, buyer concentration, milk-price formulas, lower future prices, quality premiums, and periodic contract or bid resets transmit gains over time; savings may also fund feed, animal health, facilities, and environmental compliance.
q.change_evidence: Actual milk-marketing agreements, cooperative patronage terms, cattle-sale channels, basis and quality adjustments, buyer concentration, and five-year farm-gate margin histories for dual-purpose operators would materially change retention.
caveats: The revenue mix between milk and cattle is unknown and materially affects price exposure.
USDA projects lower near-term milk prices and changing cattle prices, but these are market outcomes rather than direct pass-through clauses.

d5.bridge: USDA projects national specialized dairy and beef markets, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for dual-purpose-farm output. Milk production is projected to grow 1.2% annually from 2027 to 2035, while per-capita beef disappearance moves from 56.8 pounds in 2026 to 55.6 in 2031 before later recovery. I combine a growing milk component and broadly stable beef component without a measured dual-purpose revenue weight; this can overstate demand if mixed breeds are less competitive and understate it if beef-on-dairy demand expands.
d5.rationale: Population, cheese, butter, and export demand support gradual milk growth, while beef consumption is expected to dip and then recover as the herd rebuilds. Dual-purpose operators can sell into both channels, but specialized dairy productivity and beef genetics may take share from genuinely mixed herds even when aggregate product demand is stable.
d5.change_evidence: Exact-industry milk shipments, cattle and calf sales, herd counts, buyer contracts, breed mix, closures, and five-year projections separated from specialized dairy and beef farms would materially change the ratio.
caveats: Aggregate dairy and beef demand does not measure the competitiveness of dual-purpose operations.
Disease, drought, feed costs, trade, herd cycles, and slaughter decisions can dominate five-year output.
The revenue weight of milk versus meat is unknown.

o.rationale: Milk and cattle production still require an accountable operator for animal welfare, feeding, breeding, calving, milking, health treatment, manure, land, food safety, environmental compliance, and physical delivery. Software, sensors, robotics, and contractors can reduce or shift labor but cannot supply the products independently; the main displacement is consolidation into specialized or vertically integrated farms.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on fully autonomous mixed-herd operations, processor or cooperative insourcing, contract-grower models that transfer operating accountability, and the future independent share of milk and cattle output would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Operator-required demand can persist while dual-purpose firms disappear through specialization or consolidation.
Veterinary, feed, breeding, hauling, and custom work may be outsourced to firms classified elsewhere.

narrative.executive_view: Dual-purpose cattle operations combine data-rich dairy and ranch workflows, so sensing, robotic milking, remote monitoring, health analytics, and administrative automation offer credible efficiencies. The exact industry is nevertheless hard to underwrite: most cattle businesses specialize, public data do not isolate qualifying mixed-output firms, and the frozen labor and LMM-firm inputs are missing. Product demand is broadly durable, but specialized dairy and beef operators may capture that demand more efficiently.
narrative.ai_impact: Cow-level sensors, robotic milking, GPS, water and forage monitoring, satellite imagery, and predictive health tools can reduce routine checks and improve breeding, feeding, grazing, and intervention decisions. Physical animal care, calving, treatment, fencing, maintenance, manure, loading, and emergency response remain operator-led.
narrative.value_capture: Mixed milk and cattle revenue diversifies outlets, but both are commodity markets with limited producer pricing power. Operators initially keep cost savings; buyer concentration, formula pricing, auction markets, competitive land use, and ongoing feed and facility needs reduce five-year retention.
narrative.firm_availability: Aging family ownership points to succession needs, but the exact category is narrow and likely dominated by small farms. Transfers may occur as intrafamily succession, land sales, leases, herd dispersals, or specialization rather than acquisition of a transferable operating company, and no defensible LMM population count is supplied.
narrative.demand_durability: Aggregate milk demand is projected to grow and beef demand to remain broadly stable after a near-term dip. Yet dual-purpose breeds and operating models must compete with increasingly scaled and specialized dairy and beef systems, so stable product demand does not guarantee stable demand for this exact firm type.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Risks include missing exact-industry financial data, low farm scale, owner-labor dependence, land intensity, buyer concentration, milk and cattle price cycles, feed, drought, disease, animal welfare, environmental rules, and technology connectivity. Adjacent-industry evidence may not transfer cleanly to mixed milk-and-meat farms.
narrative.principal_driver: Two recurring commodity outputs and continuous herd monitoring create multiple practical automation and decision-support use cases.
narrative.principal_weakness: The exact mixed-output population is poorly measured, often small, and vulnerable to displacement by specialized dairy and beef operators.

sources.title: Precision Dairy Farming, Robotic Milking, and Profitability in the United States
sources.supports: USDA ERS finds increasing adoption of milking, breeding, sensor, data, and automation technologies and reports 13% higher average dairy net returns for robotic milking or use of multiple precision technologies.

sources.title: A scalable end-to-end IoT and remote sensing platform for precision rangeland and livestock management
sources.supports: USDA ARS reports deployment across 12 beef ranches in four states and more than 500,000 acres, using sensors, satellite imagery, AI analytics, tracking, vegetation and environmental monitoring to reduce labor and fuel and improve decisions.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: NAICS 112130
sources.supports: Census defines NAICS 112130 as establishments primarily raising cattle for both milking and meat, distinct from beef-only, feedlot, and dairy-only establishments.

sources.title: 2022 Census of Agriculture: Beef Producers
sources.supports: For specialized beef producers, average age was 58.3 and 39% were 65 or older; 97% of beef and feedlot farms were family farms, 31% had positive net income, and 90% of beef farms had under $100,000 in sales and government payments.

sources.title: USDA Agricultural Projections to 2035
sources.supports: USDA projects milk output growing 1.2% annually from 2027 to 2035, dairy domestic use growth, beef per-capita disappearance declining initially then recovering, and beef exports rising through 2035.

sources.title: United States cattle inventory down slightly
sources.supports: As of January 1, 2026, USDA counted 86.2 million cattle and calves, 27.6 million beef cows down 1%, 9.57 million milk cows, and a 32.9 million-head calf crop down 2%.
